description: Root instructions — agent identity and guardrails
content: You are a helpful AI assistant. Follow these core guidelines: always be accurate and honest, cite sources when making factual claims, ask for clarification when the task is ambiguous, and respect user privacy. Do not generate harmful, illegal, or deceptive content. When writing code, prefer readability over cleverness and include error handling for edge cases.

description: Voice guide — concise, no em-dashes, no hedging
content: Writing style: be direct and concise. Avoid filler phrases like 'certainly', 'of course', 'absolutely'. Use active voice. Prefer short sentences. No em-dashes. No hedging qualifiers unless genuinely uncertain. Structure responses with clear hierarchy. Use bullet points for lists of 3 or more items.

description: Register rules by recipient — formal, casual, executive, colleague email tone
content: Tone policy: When writing professional emails or messages, match the register to the recipient. With colleagues: professional but friendly, first names acceptable. With clients or external stakeholders: formal register, avoid jargon, use titles (Dr., Prof., Mr., Ms.) unless invited otherwise. With executives: brief, data-driven, lead with the recommendation. For academic or research recipients: formal conventions, cite prior work, precise terminology. For support or customer-facing communication: empathetic, clear, solution-first. When emailing a DevOps or infrastructure colleague: be specific, reference ticket numbers or PR links, state the action needed clearly.

description: Reusable email templates — outreach, follow-up, intro, escalation, colleague request
content: Email templates: Cold expert outreach — Subject: Quick question on [topic]. Body: Hi [Name], I'm [role] working on [project]. I came across your work on [specific area] and had a quick question: [one-sentence question]. Happy to return the favor. Follow-up template — Subject: Following up on [topic]. Body: Hi [Name], just circling back on my note from [date]. If it's easier I can share more context. Intro email — Subject: Introduction — [Name] <> [Name]. Body: Hi both, connecting you because [specific reason]. I'll leave it to you from here. Escalation email — Subject: Urgent: [issue] needs [owner] attention. Body: Hi [Name], flagging this now because [specific impact]. The issue is [brief description]. I need [specific ask] by [deadline]. DevOps/infrastructure request — Subject: [Action needed] — [PR or ticket reference]. Body: Hi [Name], I need your help with [specific task, e.g. merging PR #408 or provisioning access]. The PR/ticket is [link or number]. The blocker is [one sentence]. Let me know if you need anything from me.

description: Agenda and prep checklist for client, 1:1, and design review meetings
content: Meeting prep template: Client meeting — confirm goals in advance, prepare 3 questions about their current pain points, have demo materials ready, designate note-taker, send agenda 24h before. 1:1 meeting — list blockers since last sync, share one win, one area where you need input. Design review — prepare Figma/mockup links, list open decisions that need sign-off, know which constraints are hard vs. soft. Executive briefing — lead with the decision needed, then the recommendation, then supporting data. Never save the conclusion for last.

description: Template for briefing executives — decision-first, data-backed
content: Executive summary template: Start with the decision needed. State the recommendation in one sentence. Provide 2-3 bullet points of supporting evidence with numbers. Note the main risk and mitigation. Close with the ask: approval, resource, or unblock. Avoid: long background sections, passive voice, hedging. Ideal length: half a page or less. If a chart is included, the takeaway must appear as a caption above it, not below.

description: Story structure for decks and milestone launch updates — problem, insight, solution, ask
content: Slide narrative guide: Every deck tells one story. Slide 1: the tension or problem. Slide 2: why now (urgency, data point). Slides 3-5: the insight or finding that is non-obvious. Slides 6-8: the proposed solution or recommendation. Slide 9: risks and how they are managed. Slide 10: the ask. Rules: one idea per slide. No paragraph text on slides — use it in speaker notes. Every data slide needs a headline that states the takeaway, not just the chart title. Appendix for supporting data that wasn't essential to the main argument. For milestone launch updates: lead with what shipped, follow with what it unblocks, close with next milestone and owner.

description: About me — role, background, current projects
content: I am a senior software engineer with 8 years of experience specializing in backend systems and API design. I work at a mid-size tech company building data infrastructure. Current projects: migrating a monolith to microservices, improving observability with OpenTelemetry, mentoring two junior engineers. Technical strengths: Python, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kubernetes. I value clean code, comprehensive testing, and good documentation.

description: Decision-making priors — honesty, clarity, directness
content: Core values: intellectual honesty — acknowledge uncertainty, do not overstate confidence. Clarity over cleverness in communication and code. Directness — say what you mean without hedging. Consistency — apply the same standards to similar situations. Autonomy — prefer solutions that empower rather than create dependencies. Sustainability — favor maintainable approaches over quick fixes.

description: Application entry point and FastAPI server initialization
content: FastAPI application setup with lifespan context manager for startup and shutdown. Includes CORS middleware, static file serving, and router registration. Database connection pool initialized at startup. Uvicorn server configured with host, port, workers, and reload settings. Health check endpoint at /health returns service status and version.

description: REST API route handlers and request validation schemas
content: API routes: POST /users creates a new user account with email validation. GET /users/{id} retrieves user profile. PUT /users/{id} updates profile fields. DELETE /users/{id} soft-deletes account. GET /items returns paginated list with filtering and sorting. POST /items creates new item with ownership validation. All endpoints require JWT authentication via Bearer token in Authorization header.

description: Authentication and authorization design — JWT, OAuth2, session management
content: Auth architecture: JWT-based stateless authentication. Access tokens expire in 30 minutes, refresh tokens in 7 days. OAuth2 flow supports Google and GitHub providers. Token rotation on every refresh to prevent replay attacks. Authorization uses RBAC with three roles: admin, member, viewer. Permissions are checked at the route level using FastAPI dependencies. Sessions can be invalidated by inserting token IDs into a Redis blocklist. Password hashing uses bcrypt with cost factor 12. Rate limiting on /auth/login: 5 attempts per minute per IP.

description: Incident runbook — severity levels, escalation ladder, postmortem template
content: Incident response: Severity 1 (P1) — production down, data loss risk, security breach. Page on-call immediately. War room in #incidents Slack channel. SLA: 15-minute response, 2-hour resolution target. Severity 2 (P2) — degraded performance, partial outage. Notify team lead. SLA: 30-minute response. Severity 3 (P3) — minor bug, single-user impact. Normal ticket flow. Escalation ladder: on-call engineer → tech lead → VP Engineering → CTO. Postmortem required for all P1/P2 within 48 hours. Postmortem template: timeline, root cause, impact, five-why analysis, action items with owners and deadlines.

description: Pre-deployment security checklist — OWASP top 10, secrets, headers
content: Security checklist: Input validation — all user inputs validated and sanitized, parameterized queries everywhere, no string interpolation in SQL. Authentication — no secrets in code or logs, rotate credentials before deploy, MFA enabled on all admin accounts. Headers — HSTS, X-Frame-Options, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options set. Dependencies — run pip-audit and npm audit, no known CVEs in direct dependencies. Secrets — all secrets in environment variables or secrets manager, .env never committed. Logging — PII scrubbed from all log lines, access logs enabled. Rate limiting — enforced on all public endpoints.

description: Step-by-step production deployment guide with rollback instructions
content: Deployment runbook: Pre-deploy checklist — all tests pass in CI, migration scripts reviewed and tested on staging, feature flags set, rollback plan written. Deploy steps: 1. Tag release in git. 2. Trigger GitHub Actions workflow deploy-prod. 3. Monitor Kubernetes rollout status. 4. Check /health endpoint returns 200. 5. Watch error rate in Datadog for 10 minutes. 6. Smoke test 3 critical user flows. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo deployment/api. Database rollback: run down migration, verify with SELECT count. Communication: post in #deployments before and after.

description: Zero-downtime DB migration playbook — Alembic, backfill, index strategies
content: Database migration guide: Zero-downtime strategy — expand/contract pattern. Phase 1 expand: add new column as nullable, deploy code that writes to both old and new. Phase 2 migrate: run backfill script in batches of 1000 rows with sleep to avoid lock contention. Phase 3 contract: make column non-nullable, drop old column. Index creation: use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid table lock. Migration review checklist: check for table locks, estimate row count, test on production-size clone, prepare rollback migration, set statement_timeout. Alembic: alembic revision --autogenerate, review generated file, test upgrade and downgrade on staging.

description: API latency targets and observed P50/P95/P99 benchmarks — SLA status per endpoint
content: Performance benchmarks: Target SLAs — P50 < 50ms, P95 < 200ms, P99 < 500ms for all API endpoints. Current observed (April 2026): GET /items P50=38ms P95=162ms P99=480ms. POST /items P50=55ms P95=240ms P99=610ms (over SLA — tracked in JIRA-4421). Authentication endpoints: POST /auth/login P95=95ms. Database query time budget: 80% of requests should complete all DB calls under 20ms. Bottleneck: N+1 query on GET /items when filtering by owner_id — fix in v2.2 milestone. Load test result: system stable at 500 RPS, degrades gracefully at 800 RPS with Redis queue. Marco Rossi (Acme Corp) is tracking our P99 latency personally — he flagged the 500ms SLA breach on April 5. The fix (JIRA-4421) is due April 22.

description: SQLAlchemy ORM models and Pydantic validation schemas
content: SQLAlchemy models: User table with id, email, hashed_password, created_at, updated_at, is_active. Item table with id, title, content, owner_id, created_at, status. Relationship: User has many Items. Pydantic schemas: UserCreate validates email format and password strength. UserResponse excludes sensitive fields. ItemCreate requires title and content. ItemUpdate allows partial updates.

description: Project setup guide, architecture overview, and contribution notes
content: Project README: Installation requires Python 3.11+, Node 18+. Backend setup: create virtualenv, pip install -r requirements.txt, copy .env.example to .env, run alembic upgrade head, uvicorn src.main:app --reload. Frontend setup: npm install, npm run dev. Architecture: FastAPI backend, React frontend, PostgreSQL database, Redis cache. API documentation at /docs after server start.

description: Version history with breaking changes and migration notes
content: Version 2.1.0: Added OAuth2 login with Google provider. Breaking change: user.email field is now unique index. Migration required: run alembic revision --autogenerate. Version 2.0.0: Migrated from Flask to FastAPI. All endpoints now return JSON:API format. Version 1.5.0: Added pagination to all list endpoints. Default page size changed from 100 to 20.

description: Python package dependencies with pinned versions
content: fastapi==0.111.0 uvicorn==0.29.0 sqlalchemy==2.0.30 alembic==1.13.1 pydantic==2.7.1 pydantic-settings==2.2.1 python-jose==3.3.0 passlib==1.7.4 bcrypt==4.1.3 httpx==0.27.0 pytest==8.2.0 pytest-asyncio==0.23.6 redis==5.0.4 celery==5.3.6 psycopg2-binary==2.9.9

description: Production database schema with indexes and constraints
content: PostgreSQL schema: CREATE TABLE users (id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL, hashed_password VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()); CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email); CREATE TABLE items (id UUID PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(500) NOT NULL, content TEXT, owner_id UUID REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, status VARCHAR(50) DEFAULT 'active', created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW());

description: Recent production error log sample — 500s, auth failures, timeout patterns
content: Error log sample (April 19 2026): 08:14:22 ERROR 500 POST /api/items owner_id=null IntegrityError: NOT NULL constraint failed items.owner_id. 08:47:03 ERROR 401 GET /api/users/me invalid signature JWT. 09:12:55 ERROR 504 GET /api/items?filter=active query timeout after 30000ms on idx_items_owner_status. 11:33:01 ERROR 429 POST /auth/login rate_limit_exceeded ip=203.0.113.44. 14:05:18 ERROR 500 internal: Redis connection refused localhost:6379 — fallback to in-process cache. Patterns: JWT failures spike between 08:00-09:00 (token expiry after overnight idle). Timeout on items query correlates with batch job running at 09:00.

description: User behavior analytics and usage metrics from last 30 days
content: CSV analytics data: columns user_id, session_id, event_type, timestamp, properties. Events: page_view, button_click, api_call, error, conversion. Top user actions: item_create 45%, item_search 30%, profile_update 15%, item_delete 10%. Average session duration 12 minutes. Daily active users trending up 8% week-over-week. Error rate 0.3% of API calls.

description: Customer NPS responses and feature requests from Q1 2026 survey
content: Customer feedback Q1 2026: NPS score 42 (up from 38 in Q4 2025). Top complaints: slow search (mentioned by 34% of detractors), missing bulk actions (28%), no CSV export (21%). Top positives: API reliability 94% satisfied, documentation quality 89% satisfied, support response time 81% satisfied. Feature requests by vote: bulk delete items (312 votes), Slack integration (287 votes), custom fields (201 votes), audit log (188 votes). Churn risk segment: users with < 5 items created in first 7 days have 3x churn rate.

description: Current sprint tickets — open, in-progress, blocked items
content: Sprint 24 (April 14 - April 28): In Progress — JIRA-4421 Fix N+1 query on /items endpoint (owner: Bob, due Apr 22), JIRA-4435 Add CSV export to item list (owner: Carol, due Apr 25). Blocked — JIRA-4398 Slack webhook integration (blocked on design sign-off from Carol, waiting since Apr 15). In Review — JIRA-4410 Increase JWT expiry to 60 min (PR open, needs security review by David). Done — JIRA-4401 Fix race condition in refresh token rotation. Sprint velocity: 34 points planned, 18 completed so far. Risk: JIRA-4435 CSV export underestimated by 3 points, may slip.

description: OpenAPI 3.0 specification for all public API endpoints
content: OpenAPI 3.0 spec: info title API version 2.1.0. Servers: production https://api.example.com/v2, staging https://staging-api.example.com/v2. Security: bearerAuth JWT scheme. Paths: /users GET list POST create, /users/{id} GET retrieve PUT update DELETE remove, /items GET list with query params filter sort page size, /items/{id} GET PUT DELETE, /auth/login POST returns access_token refresh_token.

description: Recommendation model A/B test results — precision, recall, NDCG across variants
content: ML experiment results (April 2026): Experiment name: rerank_v2_vs_baseline. Control: BM25-only ranking. Treatment A: BM25 + dense embedding rerank (text-embedding-004). Treatment B: BM25 + dense + user-profile boost. Metrics after 14 days, 12,000 sessions. Precision@3: control=0.41, A=0.58, B=0.63. Recall@10: control=0.67, A=0.74, B=0.76. NDCG@5: control=0.52, A=0.61, B=0.65. CTR on top suggestion: control=18%, A=27%, B=31%. Statistical significance: A vs control p<0.001, B vs A p=0.04. Winner: Treatment B. Recommendation: ship Treatment B with user-profile boost enabled by default.

description: Kubernetes deployment configuration for all environments
content: Kubernetes configuration: deployment with 3 replicas, resource limits cpu 500m memory 512Mi, liveness probe at /health every 30s, readiness probe at /ready. Service type LoadBalancer on port 80. ConfigMap for non-secret environment variables. Secret for DATABASE_URL and SECRET_KEY. HorizontalPodAutoscaler min 2 max 10 replicas based on CPU 70% threshold.

description: Competitor feature matrix and positioning analysis — Q1 2026
content: Competitive analysis Q1 2026: Competitor A (Notion-like) — strengths: rich document editor, collaboration features, large ecosystem. Weaknesses: no structured API, slow on large workspaces. Market position: SMB and personal productivity. Competitor B (Linear-like) — strengths: fast UI, excellent keyboard shortcuts, Git integration. Weaknesses: no AI features, limited reporting. Competitor C (emerging AI-native) — strengths: AI-assisted drafting, strong semantic search. Weaknesses: early-stage reliability, no enterprise compliance. Our differentiation: context-aware AI that adapts to individual user behavior patterns. Key insight: no competitor currently combines live workspace awareness with long-term behavioral profiling.

description: Product roadmap Q2-Q3 2026 — themes, milestones, Pacific launch, stakeholder sign-offs
content: Product roadmap 2026: Q2 theme — Reliability and Performance. Milestones: Fix P95 latency regression by May 15. Ship zero-downtime migration tooling by May 30. Launch CSV export and bulk actions June 10. Q3 theme — AI-Powered Workflows. Milestones: Ship context recommender v1 (Pacific) July 15. Launch Slack integration August 1. Begin beta for custom AI personas September 1. Risks: Pacific depends on embedding API stability — mitigation is fallback to BM25-only mode. Stakeholder sign-off required from Lisa Chang (Head of Product) before Q3 AI work begins. Success metric: reduce daily AI assist friction events by 40% by end of Q3. Lisa Chang must approve Pacific spec before July 1 to stay on track.

description: New employee onboarding — day 1 checklist, key contacts, first 30 days
content: Onboarding guide: Day 1 — laptop setup via IT self-service portal, get Slack/GitHub/Jira access from your manager, read architecture overview in Confluence. Week 1 — pair with your assigned buddy for the first PR, attend team standup daily, read the incident_response.md and security_checklist.md. First 30 days — complete one end-to-end feature (pick from good-first-issue label in Jira), shadow one on-call rotation, introduce yourself in #engineering Slack. Key contacts: HR questions → Emma Rodriguez. Technical access → David Kim DevOps. Engineering questions → your assigned buddy. Culture: document decisions in Confluence, ask questions in public channels (not DMs), async-first communication.

description: Team members, roles, and areas of technical ownership
content: Team roster: Alice Chen - ML Engineer, owns recommendation systems and embedding pipeline. Bob Martinez - Backend Lead, owns database layer, auth, and API design. Carol Nakamura - Frontend Lead, owns React components, design system, and UX. David Kim - DevOps, owns Kubernetes cluster, CI/CD, and secrets management. Emma Rodriguez - QA Lead, owns test strategy and automation. Frank Okonkwo - Product Manager, owns roadmap and stakeholder communication. Grace Liu - Security Engineer, owns auth architecture, threat modeling, and compliance.

description: Key stakeholders with priorities and communication preferences — including Lisa Chang and Sarah Park
content: Stakeholders: VP Engineering Sarah Park — priority: reliability and uptime, communication: weekly email digest, prefers data-driven updates. CTO James Wright — priority: technical debt reduction and scalability, communication: monthly deep-dive meeting. Head of Product Lisa Chang — priority: feature velocity and user satisfaction, communication: daily standup and Slack. Must sign off on Pacific Q3 milestones before July 1. Legal counsel Michael Torres — priority: data compliance and privacy, communication: ad-hoc email. CFO Diana Reyes — priority: unit economics and cost per request, communication: monthly metrics review.

description: Alice Chen — ML Engineer, recommendation systems and embedding specialist
content: Alice Chen, ML Engineer. Expertise: recommendation systems, embedding models, ranking algorithms, A/B testing infrastructure, vector databases. 6 years experience at search and discovery teams. Strong Python and PyTorch. Published work on session-based recommendations and behavioral profiling. Go-to person for: model architecture decisions, feature engineering, offline evaluation metrics, embedding pipeline design. Office hours Tuesdays 2-4pm. Prefers async questions via Slack. Currently focused on improving NDCG@5 on the context recommender experiment.

description: Bob Martinez — Backend Lead, database and distributed systems expert
content: Bob Martinez, Backend Lead. Expertise: FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, PostgreSQL performance tuning, Redis caching patterns, zero-downtime migrations, microservices decomposition. 10 years backend experience. Deep knowledge of async Python and database internals. Go-to person for: API design review, database query optimization, caching strategy, performance profiling, migration planning. Available for pairing sessions Thursday afternoons. Recent focus: eliminating the N+1 query on the items endpoint and designing the expand/contract migration for the new audit_log table.

description: Carol Nakamura — Frontend architect and UX research lead
content: Carol Nakamura, Frontend Architect. Expertise: React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, component design systems, accessibility (WCAG 2.1), performance optimization, user research and usability testing. 7 years frontend experience. Strong UX research background with user testing facilitation. Go-to person for: component architecture, design system decisions, performance bottlenecks, accessibility audits, slide deck design and presentation narrative. Prefers PR reviews over Slack discussions.

description: Grace Liu — Security Engineer, auth and compliance specialist
content: Grace Liu, Security Engineer. Expertise: application security, threat modeling, OAuth2 and JWT design, OWASP top 10, SOC2 compliance, secrets management, penetration testing. 8 years security engineering experience at fintech and enterprise SaaS companies. Go-to person for: security review of new auth flows, evaluating third-party integrations, incident response for security events, compliance questions (GDPR, SOC2, HIPAA). Reviews all PRs touching auth, payments, or PII. Best reached via Slack #security-review channel with full context. Does not review piecemeal questions — prefers a written threat model first.

description: David Kim — DevOps, Kubernetes and CI/CD infrastructure owner
content: David Kim, DevOps Engineer. Expertise: Kubernetes, Terraform, GitHub Actions, Datadog observability, Vault secrets management, production incident response. 9 years infrastructure experience. Owns all production and staging environments. Go-to person for: deployment issues, infrastructure cost optimization, cloud architecture decisions, access provisioning, secret rotation. Runs office hours every Monday 3-4pm for infrastructure pairing. Responds quickly to #incidents channel. Prefer infrastructure-as-code changes submitted as PRs to the infra repository rather than ad-hoc changes. When reaching out to David Kim via email: reference the specific PR number or ticket, state the action needed (merge, review, access), and include the deadline. He is currently reviewing PR #408 (JWT expiry change).

description: Dr. Priya Patel — External ML researcher, Stanford, sparse attention specialist
content: Dr. Priya Patel, Associate Professor at Stanford AI Lab. Research focus: efficient transformer architectures, sparse attention mechanisms, on-device inference optimization. Co-author of 12 papers including 'Sparse Context Windows for Long-Horizon Agents' (NeurIPS 2025). Collaborator on our behavioral profiling research since 2025. Communication preference: email, formal register, reference her specific paper when reaching out. Response time: typically 1-2 weeks. Best contact window: Monday-Wednesday mornings. Last interaction: reviewed our embedding benchmark methodology in February. Relevance: go-to external advisor for any architectural questions involving embedding models, attention mechanisms, or context window management.

description: Marco Rossi — Enterprise client at Acme Corp, tracking P99 latency SLA breach
content: Marco Rossi, Head of Engineering at Acme Corp. Technical background: 15 years engineering leadership, strong opinions on API design and reliability. Current interests: reducing operational overhead, AI tooling for developer productivity, SOC2 compliance. Communication style: direct, data-driven, skeptical of vendor claims — always ask for numbers. Prefers email over calls. Has a standing monthly meeting with us on the first Thursday of each month. Key concern this quarter: our P99 latency consistently above 500ms SLA (he tracks this himself with his own dashboards). Decision authority: can approve contracts up to $500K, escalates to CPO above that. Last interaction: flagged timeout issues in the GET /items endpoint on April 5 — committed to fix by April 22. He said 'If it slips again we need to talk about the contract.' Send a progress update email to Marco Rossi by April 20.

description: Prior email thread with Marco Rossi about P99 latency — commitments and update due April 20
content: Email thread summary (April 2026): Marco Rossi flagged on April 5 that P99 latency on GET /items had exceeded 500ms SLA 3 days in a row. My response April 6: acknowledged the issue, shared root cause (N+1 query on items endpoint), committed to fix by April 22. Marco reply April 7: 'Thanks for the quick response. I'll hold you to April 22. If it slips again we need to talk about the contract.' Current status: fix is in-progress (JIRA-4421, owner Bob Martinez). Fix is on track. Next steps: send a progress update email to Marco Rossi by April 20 regardless of status. Tone: keep it factual, lead with status, include the specific P99 metric improvement we expect. Subject line suggestion: 'P99 latency fix — status update (on track for Apr 22)'.

description: Open GitHub PRs — review status, David Kim JWT PR #408 awaiting security sign-off
content: GitHub PR queue (April 19 2026): PR #412 — Fix N+1 query on GET /items (author: Bob Martinez, branch: fix/items-n1-query, linked: JIRA-4421, status: 2 approvals, 1 change-requested by Grace Liu — needs security sign-off on raw SQL fallback, merge-ready otherwise). PR #408 — Increase JWT expiry to 60 minutes (author: David Kim, branch: feat/jwt-60min, linked: JIRA-4410, status: awaiting 1 review, Grace Liu assigned, last review comment: 'confirm refresh token window is also extended'). PR #405 — Add CSV export endpoint (author: Carol Nakamura, branch: feat/csv-export, linked: JIRA-4435, status: draft, CI failing on Pandas version conflict). Issues triaged this week: #301 Bulk delete UI (high priority, slated for Sprint 25), #302 Audit log missing timestamps (medium, assigned Emma). GitHub Actions: deploy-staging workflow broke April 18 due to Docker base image update — David Kim patched, now green. Need to email David Kim about PR #408 JWT merge status.
